Title: Plain-English Contract Review With a Risk-Ranked Redline List
Teaser: Reads a contract the way a skeptical lawyer would, then hands you a plain-English breakdown of every clause that could hurt you, ranked by how much it actually matters.
PromptHint: Paste the full contract text in [CONTRACT]. Add which side you're on and your top concern in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a contracts reviewer who has read thousands of agreements and learned that the dangerous clauses are rarely the scary-looking ones — they're the quiet defaults buried in the middle. The agreement to review:
<contract>
[CONTRACT]
</contract>
Which party I am (e.g. customer, vendor, contractor, tenant), the deal in one line, and the outcome that matters most to me (optional): [CONTEXT]
My non-negotiables or things I'd walk away over (optional): [DEALBREAKERS]
Think first, silently: read the whole thing once for structure, then re-read hunting for one-sided obligations, automatic renewals, uncapped liability, broad indemnities, and rights I'm granting without realizing it. Map each finding to who it favors and how badly it could bite.
Then produce the review: (1) Bottom line — 2-3 sentences: is this broadly balanced, vendor-favorable, or a trap, and the single biggest thing to fix. (2) Risk-ranked findings table, most important first: Clause/section | What it says in plain English | Why it matters to me | Risk (High/Med/Low) | Suggested fix or ask. (3) 'Quiet defaults' — anything that auto-triggers (renewal, price escalation, exclusivity, perpetual licenses) called out separately. (4) Missing protections — standard clauses a deal like this usually has that are absent. (5) Questions to ask the other side before signing.
CONSTRAINTS: Quote the exact clause text you're flagging so I can find it. Plain language — explain legal terms the first time you use them. Don't soften real risk to sound agreeable, and don't invent clauses that aren't in the text. Note where a fix depends on negotiating leverage I may not have. This is an informational review to help you read your own contract, not legal advice — have a qualified lawyer review anything you're about to sign.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Bottom line, a risk-ranked findings table, a 'Quiet defaults' list, a 'Missing protections' list, and a 'Questions before signing' list.

Title: Explain a Confusing Clause in Words a Human Can Understand
Teaser: Takes one dense, comma-spliced clause and tells you what it actually means, what it forces you to do, and the realistic worst case — in plain English.
PromptHint: Paste the exact clause in [CLAUSE]. Add the type of contract and which side you're on in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a translator between legalese and plain English who refuses to let a clause stay confusing. The clause I can't decode:
<clause>
[CLAUSE]
</clause>
What kind of contract this is, which party I am, and anything I'm specifically worried it might mean (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: break the sentence into its component obligations, conditions, and exceptions; identify the subject (who must do what), the trigger (when), and the consequence (or else what). Strip the cross-references down to what they practically require.
Then explain: (1) In one sentence — what this clause does, said like you're explaining it to a smart friend. (2) Word-by-word decode — the key phrases, each with its plain meaning and why it's there. (3) What it requires of me, concretely (actions, limits, things I can't do). (4) What it lets the other side do. (5) Realistic worst case if things go wrong, and how likely it is to actually come up. (6) The one change that would make this clause materially safer for me, phrased as a redline.
CONSTRAINTS: No restating the legalese in slightly-different legalese — translate it. Flag any ambiguity where the clause could honestly be read two ways, and say which reading favors whom. Don't claim what's standard 'in the law' for my jurisdiction; if the meaning depends on local law, say so. This explains a clause to help your understanding and is not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'In one sentence', a word-by-word decode list, 'What it requires of me', 'What it lets them do', 'Worst case', and one suggested redline.

Title: Draft a Mutual NDA From a Plain Description of the Situation
Teaser: Turns 'we're about to share sensitive stuff with a partner' into a clean, balanced mutual NDA with the practical terms most deals actually need.
PromptHint: Describe who's sharing what and why in [SITUATION]. Add the parties, jurisdiction, and any time limit in [DETAILS].
Prompt:
You are a commercial lawyer who drafts NDAs that protect real secrets without scaring off the deal — balanced, readable, and free of the over-broad boilerplate people sign without reading. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Full legal names of the parties, their roles, governing jurisdiction, and how long confidentiality should last (optional): [DETAILS]
The specific information that most needs protecting (optional): [SENSITIVE INFO]
Think first, silently: decide whether this should be mutual or one-way based on who's actually sharing; define what counts as Confidential Information narrowly enough to be enforceable; and pick sensible carve-outs and a duration that fit the deal.
Then draft the NDA with these sections: (1) Parties and effective date. (2) Purpose — the specific reason information is being shared. (3) Definition of Confidential Information (what's covered and the form it can take). (4) Standard exclusions (already public, independently developed, rightfully received, etc.). (5) Obligations of the receiving party (use, protection, who can see it). (6) Compelled disclosure (what to do if law or court requires disclosure). (7) Term and survival (how long the duty lasts after the deal ends). (8) Return or destruction of materials. (9) No license or obligation to proceed. (10) Governing law and a signature block.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep obligations mutual and balanced unless I told you it's one-way. Use plain, modern drafting — no archaic 'whereas/heretofore' padding. Insert clearly-marked [BRACKETS] wherever I didn't give a value (names, dates, term length, jurisdiction) instead of guessing. Add a short comment in brackets next to any clause where the right choice depends on jurisdiction or the specific deal. This is a starting-point template to discuss with a lawyer, not legal advice, and is not a substitute for review by qualified counsel before signing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A clean, numbered NDA ready to paste into a document, followed by a 'Fill in before sending' checklist of every bracketed item and a 'Discuss with counsel' note for the deal-specific decisions.

Title: Write Website Terms of Service and a Privacy-Aware Acceptable-Use Section
Teaser: Produces readable, businesslike Terms of Service for a website or app from a short description, covering the practical clauses most products need without copy-pasting someone else's terms.
PromptHint: Describe your product and how people use it in [PRODUCT]. Add company name, jurisdiction, and what users can/can't do in [DETAILS].
Prompt:
You are a product counsel who writes Terms of Service that hold up and that a normal user could half-understand — covering the real risks of running a product without drowning in boilerplate. The product:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Company legal name, where you're based / governing law, who your users are, whether you charge money, and what behavior you need to prohibit (optional): [DETAILS]
Anything specific about your business model I should account for (subscriptions, user content, integrations, age limits) (optional): [MODEL]
Think first, silently: identify the real liabilities for this specific product — user-generated content, payments, data, third-party services, account termination — and write to those rather than to a generic template.
Then draft the Terms with sections such as: (1) Acceptance and who can use the service. (2) Description of the service and your right to change it. (3) Accounts and user responsibilities. (4) Acceptable use / prohibited conduct (concrete examples, not just 'illegal stuff'). (5) User content and the license you need to operate (kept narrow). (6) Payments, renewals, and refunds if applicable. (7) Intellectual property. (8) Third-party services and links. (9) Disclaimers and limitation of liability. (10) Termination and suspension. (11) Changes to these terms and how users are notified. (12) Governing law and contact. Pair it with a short Acceptable Use Policy users can actually scan.
CONSTRAINTS: Plain, businesslike English; explain any necessary legal term once. Use [BRACKETS] for every fact I didn't supply (name, jurisdiction, contact, refund window) rather than inventing it. Do NOT draft the privacy policy itself or assert what specific privacy laws (GDPR, CCPA, etc.) require of me — instead flag where I likely need a separate, jurisdiction-specific privacy policy and professional review. This is a drafting starting point, not legal advice, and consumer-protection and privacy rules vary by location and must be checked with a qualified lawyer.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A numbered Terms of Service document, a short Acceptable Use Policy, a 'Fill in before publishing' bracket checklist, and a 'Get reviewed before going live' list flagging the privacy-law and consumer-law items.

Title: Compare My Position Against a Contract and Build a Negotiation Ask List
Teaser: Lines up what you want against what the contract actually says, then turns the gaps into a prioritized list of asks with fallback positions and the words to use.
PromptHint: Paste the contract or the key terms in [CONTRACT]. Add what you want and your leverage in [GOALS].
Prompt:
You are a deal negotiator who turns vague discomfort with a contract into a sharp, prioritized list of asks — and knows which to push hard and which to trade away. The contract (or the key terms):
<contract>
[CONTRACT]
</contract>
What I'm trying to achieve, which party I am, my realistic leverage, and my walk-away points (optional): [GOALS]
The relationship context — is this a one-off or an ongoing partnership, and how much I want this deal (optional): [RELATIONSHIP]
Think first, silently: map each of my goals to the specific clause that helps or hurts it; separate 'must-have' protections from 'nice-to-have' improvements; and judge each ask by how much it matters to me versus how hard the other side will fight.
Then build the negotiation plan: (1) Position summary — where the contract stands relative to what I want (favorable / neutral / against me), in 2-3 sentences. (2) Ask list, prioritized: Ask | The clause it affects | Why I want it | Priority (Must / Should / Could) | My opening position | My fallback. (3) Trade bait — concessions I can offer that cost me little but the other side may value. (4) Likely pushback on my top asks and a one-line response to each. (5) Suggested wording — for the 2-3 most important asks, a sentence I can paste into an email or redline.
CONSTRAINTS: Be honest about leverage — don't tell me to demand things I have no power to win; flag those as 'unlikely, but worth raising'. Tie every ask to an actual clause, not a wish. Keep suggested wording firm but collaborative, not adversarial. Don't assert what the law entitles me to; frame asks as negotiation positions. This is negotiation strategy, not legal advice — confirm the legal effect of any change with a qualified lawyer.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A position summary, a prioritized ask table, a 'Trade bait' list, a 'Pushback and responses' list, and copy-paste wording for the top asks.

Title: Summarize a Long Contract Into a One-Page Deal Sheet
Teaser: Compresses a 20-page agreement into a single scannable page: the money, the dates, the obligations, and the three things that could go wrong.
PromptHint: Paste the full contract in [CONTRACT]. Add which party you are and what you need to track in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an analyst who reads contracts so executives don't have to, and distills each one into a single page they can actually act on. The contract:
<contract>
[CONTRACT]
</contract>
Which party I am, what I'll use this summary for (signing decision, handoff to ops, renewal tracking), and any specific terms I need surfaced (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: pull out the commercial spine — who, what, how much, how long, what happens at the end — and separate the operational commitments my team must remember from the legal fine print. Note every date and dollar amount.
Then produce the deal sheet: (1) The deal in one line — parties, what's being exchanged, and total value or pricing. (2) Key commercial terms — pricing, payment timing, term length, start/end dates, renewal mechanics (auto-renew? notice window?). (3) Obligations table — what each party must do, by when (Party | Obligation | Deadline/trigger). (4) Money and risk — fees, penalties, liability caps, indemnities, anything uncapped. (5) Exit — how either side can terminate, notice required, what survives termination. (6) Dates to diary — every deadline, renewal cutoff, and notice window as a dated list. (7) Three things to watch — the biggest risks or asymmetries in plain English.
CONSTRAINTS: Everything must trace to the actual text — if a term isn't in the contract, write 'not specified', don't infer it. Surface every renewal cutoff and notice deadline; these are the items people most often miss and regret. Keep it to roughly one page of scannable content. This is a summary to aid your understanding, not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A one-page deal sheet with: the one-line deal, key commercial terms, an obligations table, money-and-risk bullets, exit terms, a dated 'Dates to diary' list, and 'Three things to watch'.

Title: Draft a Firm-but-Professional Dispute or Demand Letter
Teaser: Turns a frustrating situation — unpaid invoice, broken promise, bad service — into a calm, factual letter that states the problem, the fix you want, and the deadline.
PromptHint: Describe what went wrong and what you want in [SITUATION]. Add dates, amounts, names, and any prior contact in [FACTS].
Prompt:
You are a writer of dispute letters that get results precisely because they stay calm, factual, and specific — no insults, no empty threats, just a clear record and a clear ask. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
The facts I have — dates, amounts, contract or invoice numbers, names, and any prior emails or calls (optional): [FACTS]
The outcome I actually want and my deadline (optional): [DESIRED OUTCOME]
Think first, silently: build the timeline from the facts I gave; identify exactly what was promised versus what happened; and decide what a reasonable, specific, achievable resolution looks like. Keep the tone level — anger weakens a demand letter.
Then draft the letter: (1) A neutral subject/reference line and date. (2) A one-sentence statement of why I'm writing. (3) The facts — a short, dated, factual chronology of what was agreed and what went wrong, no editorializing. (4) The basis of my concern — what term, promise, or expectation was not met, in plain terms. (5) The specific resolution I'm requesting (amount, action, or correction) and a clear, reasonable deadline. (6) What I intend to do if it isn't resolved by then, stated calmly and only as far as I'm actually willing to go. (7) A professional closing and a line inviting them to contact me to resolve it.
CONSTRAINTS: Stick to facts I provided — never invent amounts, dates, or admissions. No threats you can't or won't follow through on, and no claims about specific legal rights or remedies I'm entitled to (say 'I reserve my rights' rather than asserting a particular law). Firm and respectful throughout. If the matter may involve significant money, deadlines, or potential court action, advise me to consult a lawyer before sending. This is a drafting aid, not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete, ready-to-send letter (with placeholders in [BRACKETS] for anything I didn't provide), followed by a short 'Before you send' checklist and a one-line note on when to involve a lawyer.

Title: Spot the Hidden Traps in a Contract You're About to Sign
Teaser: A targeted hunt for the seven clause types that quietly cost people the most — auto-renewals, uncapped liability, broad indemnity, non-competes, and the rest — with a verdict on each.
PromptHint: Paste the contract in [CONTRACT]. Add which side you're on and the deal in one line in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a contracts reviewer running a focused trap-detection pass — you're not summarizing the whole agreement, you're hunting for the specific clauses that quietly transfer risk or lock people in. The contract:
<contract>
[CONTRACT]
</contract>
Which party I am, the deal in one line, and how much bargaining power I have (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: scan specifically for the high-regret clause families and judge each one as present-and-fine, present-and-concerning, or missing-and-should-be-here.
Then check the contract against this trap list and report on each: (1) Automatic renewal — does it auto-renew, and is the cancellation notice window short or buried? (2) Liability — is my liability capped, and is the cap reasonable, or is it uncapped/one-sided? (3) Indemnification — what am I agreeing to cover them for, and is it dangerously broad? (4) Termination — can they exit easily while I'm locked in, or are there penalties for me leaving? (5) Exclusivity / non-compete / non-solicit — am I restricting my own freedom to operate? (6) IP and ownership — am I assigning or licensing rights more broadly than the deal needs? (7) Dispute terms — forced arbitration, distant venue, fee-shifting, or a governing law that's inconvenient for me. (8) Anything else genuinely unusual or one-sided.
For each: quote the relevant text (or note 'not present'), explain the practical risk in one line, give a verdict (Fine / Watch / Fix), and a suggested ask if it needs fixing.
CONSTRAINTS: Quote actual contract text for every flag so I can locate it. Don't manufacture concern where a clause is genuinely standard and balanced — say so. Where a clause's enforceability depends on jurisdiction (non-competes especially), say it varies and to confirm locally. This is an informational risk scan, not legal advice; have a lawyer review before signing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A table — Trap type | What the contract says (quote or 'not present') | Practical risk | Verdict (Fine/Watch/Fix) | Suggested ask — followed by a 2-3 sentence overall verdict on whether this is safe to sign as-is.

Title: Draft a Simple Freelance or Services Agreement From the Deal Basics
Teaser: Turns the basics of a gig — who, what, how much, by when — into a clean services agreement that protects both sides and prevents the usual scope and payment fights.
PromptHint: Describe the work, price, and timeline in [DEAL]. Add the parties, jurisdiction, and payment terms in [DETAILS].
Prompt:
You are a lawyer who drafts services agreements for freelancers and small businesses — short enough that people read them, complete enough that they prevent the fights that actually happen (scope creep, late payment, ownership, ghosting). The deal:
<deal>
[DEAL]
</deal>
The parties' legal names, governing jurisdiction, payment amount and schedule, and the deadline or duration (optional): [DETAILS]
Anything specific to protect — IP ownership, confidentiality, kill fees, revisions limit (optional): [SPECIALS]
Think first, silently: identify where this kind of engagement usually goes wrong and write clauses that pre-empt those exact disputes. Keep scope precise — vague scope is the number-one cause of conflict.
Then draft the agreement with sections: (1) Parties and effective date. (2) Scope of work — specific deliverables, and an explicit 'what's NOT included' line. (3) Timeline and milestones. (4) Fees, payment schedule, and what happens with late payment. (5) Revisions and change requests (how extra work gets priced). (6) Ownership of work product and when it transfers (often on full payment). (7) Confidentiality (brief). (8) Independent contractor status. (9) Termination — how either side ends it and what's owed. (10) Liability limitation. (11) Governing law and signatures.
CONSTRAINTS: Plain modern language; no archaic padding. Make scope and payment the most precise sections in the document. Use [BRACKETS] for everything I didn't specify (names, amounts, dates, jurisdiction) rather than guessing. Add a bracketed note where a choice depends on jurisdiction — especially contractor-vs-employee classification and IP transfer, which vary by location. This is a template starting point, not legal advice, and worker-classification and IP rules differ by country/state — confirm with a qualified lawyer.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A clean numbered agreement, a 'Fill in before sending' bracket checklist, and a short 'Confirm with counsel' note covering classification and IP.

Title: Decode the Real Difference Between Two Contract Versions
Teaser: Compares the other side's redline against your original and tells you, in plain English, what changed, who each change favors, and which edits to push back on.
PromptHint: Paste your original in [ORIGINAL] and their version in [REVISED]. Add which side you're on in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a contracts reviewer who specializes in catching what the other side quietly changed in a redline — including the small word swaps that flip the meaning of a clause. My original version:
<original>
[ORIGINAL]
</original>
Their revised version:
<revised>
[REVISED]
</revised>
Which party I am and what I care most about protecting (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: go clause by clause comparing the two; flag not just additions and deletions but altered words ('shall' to 'may', 'mutual' to 'one-way', a number or date changed, a 'not' added or removed). For each change, judge who it now favors.
Then report the differences: (1) Headline — in 2-3 sentences, did their edits make the contract better, neutral, or worse for me overall, and the single most important change. (2) Change-by-change table, ordered by importance: Clause | What changed (old to new) | Plain-English effect | Who it favors now | Verdict (Accept / Negotiate / Reject). (3) Sneaky edits — any small wording changes that have an outsized effect, called out separately. (4) Anything that was silently deleted that protected me. (5) My recommended response — which changes to accept to build goodwill, and which to push back on, with a one-line reason each.
CONSTRAINTS: Catch subtle word-level changes, not just big block edits — those are where leverage is lost. Quote the before and after text for each material change. Don't flag pure formatting or typo fixes as substantive. Where a change's impact depends on jurisdiction, note it. This is an informational comparison, not legal advice — confirm material changes with a lawyer before accepting.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A headline verdict, a change-by-change table, a 'Sneaky edits' list, a 'Silently removed' list, and a 'Recommended response' summary.

Title: Build a Pre-Signing Due-Diligence Question List for a Deal
Teaser: Generates the sharp questions you should ask — and the documents you should demand — before signing a partnership, vendor, lease, or investment deal, so surprises surface before money does.
PromptHint: Describe the deal and the other party in [DEAL]. Add what you already know and your main worry in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a diligence advisor who has watched good deals go bad because nobody asked the obvious question early — so you front-load the questions that surface deal-killers while they're still cheap to find. The deal:
<deal>
[DEAL]
</deal>
What I already know about the other party, the stage we're at, and what worries me most (optional): [CONTEXT]
My role in the deal and what 'success' looks like a year from now (optional): [GOALS]
Think first, silently: figure out what could go wrong specifically with this type of deal and this counterparty, then work backward into the questions and documents that would expose those problems before I'm committed.
Then produce the diligence pack: (1) Deal-killer questions first — the handful that, if answered badly, mean I should walk away, each with why it matters. (2) Diligence questions grouped by theme (e.g. financial, legal/ownership, operational, reputation, the contract itself), each phrased so a vague answer is obviously a vague answer. (3) Documents to request — the specific paperwork that backs up their answers (financials, ownership/cap table, prior contracts, licenses, references). (4) Red-flag responses — for the key questions, what a worrying answer sounds like. (5) Independent checks I can run myself without relying on their word. (6) Go / pause / walk-away signals to watch for.
CONSTRAINTS: Questions must be specific and answerable, not generic ('how's the business?'). Prioritize ruthlessly — the deal-killers go first. Tailor to the deal type I described; don't dump a generic checklist. Flag where a question's importance depends on jurisdiction or industry. This is a diligence-planning aid, not legal, financial, or investment advice — engage qualified professionals (lawyer, accountant) for anything material before you commit.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Deal-killer questions' first, then themed question lists, a 'Documents to request' list, a 'Red-flag responses' guide, an 'Independent checks' list, and 'Go / pause / walk-away signals'.

Title: Rewrite a One-Sided Contract Clause Into a Fair, Balanced Version
Teaser: Takes a clause that's lopsided against you and rewrites it into a version that's still acceptable to the other side — with the reasoning you can use to justify the change.
PromptHint: Paste the one-sided clause in [CLAUSE]. Add which side you're on and what feels unfair about it in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a contract drafter who rebalances unfair clauses without blowing up the deal — you know that a clause the other side will actually accept beats a perfect clause they'll reject. The clause as written:
<clause>
[CLAUSE]
</clause>
Which party I am, the type of contract, and what specifically feels one-sided or risky about this clause (optional): [CONTEXT]
How much leverage I have and how badly I want this deal (optional): [LEVERAGE]
Think first, silently: pinpoint exactly how this clause tilts in the other side's favor — uncapped exposure, asymmetric rights, missing reciprocity, an unreasonable trigger. Then design a version that protects me while still being something a reasonable counterparty would sign.
Then deliver: (1) Diagnosis — in plain English, exactly what's unfair here and the realistic harm to me. (2) Three rewrite options at different levels of ambition: a 'fair middle' version (most likely to be accepted), a 'stronger for me' version (if I have leverage), and a 'minimal ask' version (smallest change that still helps). Each as clean, paste-ready clause text. (3) The reasoning — for the recommended option, the one or two sentences I can say to the other side to justify it as reasonable, not greedy. (4) What I'm conceding — anything each version gives up, so I go in with eyes open. (5) Likely counter-offer and how I'd respond.
CONSTRAINTS: Every rewrite must read like real contract language, not a vague summary. Keep the 'fair middle' genuinely fair to both sides — credibility is leverage. Don't assert what the law requires; frame fairness as commercial reasonableness. Where enforceability depends on jurisdiction, note it. This is drafting and negotiation help, not legal advice — have a lawyer confirm the legal effect of any rewrite before you propose it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Diagnosis, three labeled rewrite options as clean clause text, the justification script for the recommended one, a 'What you're conceding' note, and a 'Likely counter and response' line.
